novelty.question:
Prior-art / novelty assessment for the "metallic exponent" in low-dimensional topology / character varieties. For EACH of the
3 results below decide KNOWN (cite) / PARTIALLY-KNOWN / APPEARS-NOVEL / NEEDS-SPECIALIST; be adversarial (assume known, hunt prior art).
CONTEXT: metallic once-punctured-torus bundles = mapping tori with monodromy R^m L^m (m=1 = figure-eight knot complement);
SL(n,C) character variety; cusp meridian mu=A^{-m} t; longitude [A,B]; on the geometric (loxodromic-meridian) component there is
a matrix identity [A,B]=s*mu^k; k is essentially the A-polynomial slope.
R1: the SL(n) figure-eight A-polynomial family L=(-1)^{n-1} M^n (n=2 Cooper-Long; n=3 Heusener-Munoz-Porti / Falbel; n=4 L=-M^4) and
its metallic (m>1) generalization -- is the higher-n family known? (check Bergeron-Falbel-Guilloux, Garoufalidis-Thurston-Zickert).
R2: the observation that the exponent k must be read on the order(mu)=infinity (cusped) stratum, finite-order-mu reps being
Dehn-filling/orbifold points -- is this stratification of the A-polynomial-exponent standard? (Culler-Shalen, A-polynomial literature).
R3: the exponent being ORDER-determined (by the boundary spectrum order o), NOT rank-determined, and that there is no simple k(o,m)
closed form. -- is an order/arithmetic characterization of the A-polynomial slope for punctured-torus bundles known? (Baker-Petersen
tunnel-number-one char varieties; Hoste-Shanahan twist knots).
Deliver a per-result verdict + strongest citations (authors,year,what) + residual uncertainty / what a specialist must confirm.
novelty.summary: All three results survive an adversarial prior-art hunt as APPEARS-NOVEL at the level the project claims them, with well-documented n=2 and n=3 prior art bounding them. R1 (the SL(n) figure-eight A-polynomial family L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n and its metallic m>1 generalization): the n=2 case is classical (Cooper-Long) and the n=3 case is fully known prior art (Falbel-Guilloux-Koseleff-Rouillier-Thistlethwaite arXiv:1412.4711, Heusener-Munoz-Porti arXiv:1505.04451, Zickert arXiv:1405.0025, with explicit L^3=M / L^3 M=1 relations on Dehn-filling components and an explicit geometric-component SL(3) A-polynomial), but NO source presents a uniform higher-n (n>=4) family, the L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n closed form across n, or any metallic R^m L^m (m>1) higher-n generalization; the GTZ/Goerner gluing-equation machinery (arXiv:1207.6711) is boundary-unipotent parametrization only and computes the figure-eight at n=3,4 as zero-dimensional components with no A-polynomial. R2 (exponent must be read on the order(mu)=infinity cusped stratum): the valuation-theoretic version — A-polynomial slope = ratio of valuations at ideal points = boundary slope (Newton polygon) — is fully standard (CCGLS 1994; Benard-Florens-Rodau arXiv:2103.14151), but only in rank 2, and the standard stratification is ideal-points-vs-smooth-points (valuation-theoretic), NOT the project's order(mu)=infinity-vs-finite-order-mu (Dehn-filling/orbifold) stratification, so the specific framing is PARTIALLY-KNOWN. R3 (exponent is ORDER-determined by boundary spectrum order o, not rank, with no simple k(o,m) closed form): the n=2 character-variety prior art for tunnel-number-one and twist-knot punctured-torus families is solid (Baker-Petersen arXiv:1211.4479; Petersen NYJM 2015; Hoste-Shanahan), but none gives an order/arithmetic characterization of the A-polynomial slope at higher rank, so the order-not-rank claim APPEARS-NOVEL pending specialist confirmation.

findings.claim: R1 VERDICT — PARTIALLY-KNOWN (n=2,3 known; uniform higher-n family and metallic generalization APPEARS-NOVEL). The n=3 SL(3,C)/PGL(3,C) figure-eight A-polynomial is fully documented prior art: the character variety has 5 components (3 irreducible, dim 2), the distinguished/geometric component V0 contains the Sym^2:SL(2,C)->SL(3,C) holonomy curve, and the eigenvalue relations L^3=M (component D2) and L^3 M=1 / L^3=1/M (component D3) hold on the Dehn-filling components, with an explicit SL(3) A-polynomial on the geometric component matching Zickert. This establishes the n=3 instance of L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n. NO source presents the uniform higher-n (n>=4) family, the closed-form L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n across n, or any metallic (R^m L^m, m>1) higher-n generalization.
findings.evidence: Falbel et al. (arXiv:1412.4711, Exp. Math. 2016) state verbatim L^3=M on D2 and L^3 M=1 on D3, plus an explicit geometric-component A-polynomial (-L^8 M + 2L^7 M + 3L^6 M - ... - M = 0) 'the same as the one independently found by C. Zickert'. Heusener-Munoz-Porti (arXiv:1505.04451, Illinois J. Math 2016) confirm 5 components dim 2 with the distinguished component containing Sym^2 of the SL(2) holonomy (= geometric/loxodromic-meridian component for n=3). GTZ/Goerner (arXiv:1207.6711, AGT 2015) compute figure-eight only at n=3 (full) and n=4 (gluing equations only) as ZERO-dimensional boundary-unipotent components over Q(sqrt(-7))/Q(sqrt(-3)), explicitly 'not the standard meridian and longitude' — no A-polynomial, no L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n. NUANCE: the tidy cubic L^3=M lives on Dehn-filling components D2/D3; the geometric component D1's eigenvalue relation is a 141-polynomial Grobner basis with no tidy form (relevant to R2).
findings.vote: 5 supporting claims [0,1,2,3,4,5,6], all primary peer-reviewed sources

findings.claim: R1 corroboration — the geometric vocabulary (meridian acting as monodromy by conjugation, longitude = fiber-boundary commutator [a,b]) used in the metallic framework is standard for once-punctured-torus bundles, and the metallic R^m L^m family (figure-eight = m=1) is a recognized special case of the Murasugi R/L word decomposition. But no higher-n A-polynomial construction exists in this peripheral-setup literature.
findings.evidence: Daly (arXiv:2411.04431, Nov 2024) sets up pi_1(M) = <a,b,x | xax^-1=phi(a), xbx^-1=phi(b)>, with x a meridian acting as monodromy phi, and longitude l = aba^-1 b^-1 = [a,b]; uses Murasugi decomposition phi = i^e R^{n1} L^{n2}... so R^m L^m is a special case and the figure-eight (m=1) is the precursor. This matches the metallic mu=A^{-m}t / [A,B] vocabulary at the vocabulary level (mu=A^{-m}t is a twisted meridian, a standard peripheral adjustment). The paper is projective-rigidity via twisted Alexander polynomial, not an SL(n) A-polynomial tower.
findings.vote: 1 supporting claim [7], primary source; the 'PGL(4,R) projective-deformation = R^mL^m setup' framing claim was REFUTED 0-3 as overreaching toward prior-art status, leaving only the vocabulary-level match

findings.claim: R2 VERDICT — PARTIALLY-KNOWN. Reading the A-polynomial slope/exponent at the ideal (cusped, pole-at-infinity) stratum is fully standard: the slope invariant equals minus the logarithmic Gauss map of the A-polynomial, and at an ideal point equals the ratio of valuations v_y(L)/v_y(M) = minus the Culler-Shalen boundary slope = slope of the corresponding Newton-polygon side (CCGLS 1994). BUT this standard stratification is ideal-points-vs-smooth-points (valuation-theoretic), NOT the project's order(mu)=infinity-vs-finite-order-mu (cusped-vs-Dehn-filling/orbifold) stratification, and it is strictly rank 2.
findings.evidence: Benard-Florens-Rodau (arXiv:2103.14151, 2021) Theorem 4.1: s_K(chi) = -(M/L)(d_M A / d_L A) on each dim-1 component; Theorem 1.3: slope = minus the log-Gauss map of A; Theorem 5.2 (at ideal point y): slope = v_y(L)/v_y(M) = minus boundary slope of the Culler-Shalen incompressible surface = slope of the Newton-polygon side (foundational CCGLS Inventiones 118, 1994). Strictly SL_2(C) (abstract). The paper's stratification is ideal (pole) vs smooth (regular) points — valuation-theoretic — which the verifier explicitly flagged as NOT identical to R2's order(mu)=infinity vs finite-order-mu stratification. CAVEAT from R1 finding: Falbel's tidy L^3=M relations appear on the Dehn-filling components while the geometric component carries the complicated relation — the inverse of the n=2 reading-on-geometric-component picture, a genuine open framing wrinkle for R2.
findings.vote: 2 supporting claims [9,10], primary source; FOUR adjacent CCGLS/Thurston-cone stratification claims were REFUTED (0-3 / 1-2) for asserting the order/finite-vs-infinite stratification is 'standard' when the literature framing is valuation-theoretic or geometric-cone, not meridian-order

findings.claim: R3 VERDICT — APPEARS-NOVEL (order-not-rank characterization and absence of simple k(o,m)). The n=2 character-variety prior art for the relevant punctured-torus-bundle / twist-knot families is solid and well-cited, but none provides an order/arithmetic characterization of the A-polynomial slope, and all are confined to rank 2 — so no prior art contradicts the order-determined (vs rank-determined) exponent claim or the absence of a simple k(o,m) closed form.
findings.evidence: Baker-Petersen (arXiv:1211.4479, IJM 2013) determine the full SL_2(C)/PSL_2(C) character varieties of tunnel-number-one once-punctured-torus bundles (Whitehead-link fillings) with birational/smooth models and canonical-component genera — direct n=2 char-variety prior art, but purely SL_2 (0 SL(n>2), 0 A-polynomial mentions). Petersen (NYJM 21, 2015) computes A-polynomials of J(k,l) double-twist knots strictly via the SL_2(C) eigenvalue map; Hoste-Shanahan place figure-eight = J(2,-2) inside the two-bridge/twist-knot tunnel-number-one family. NUANCE: tunnel-number-one is the single-twist R L^n family; the metallic R^m L^m (m>=2) is NOT tunnel-number-one — figure-eight (m=1) is the unique overlap — so even at n=2 the metallic family m>=2 sits just outside the Baker-Petersen coverage. None of these gives an order/arithmetic slope characterization at any rank.
findings.vote: 4 supporting claims [11,12,13,14], all primary peer-reviewed sources, unanimous except [11] at 2-1

novelty.caveats: SCOPE OF EVIDENCE: This synthesis confirms what the cited literature does and does NOT contain; it does not independently re-verify the project's own metallic-exponent computations (k=4-m(o-3) etc., which per memory B157 was itself REFUTED in-project, with 'order-not-rank' surviving). The novelty verdicts are therefore 'no contradicting prior art found,' not 'proven novel.' STRATIFICATION MISMATCH (R2): the strongest residual uncertainty — the standard literature stratifies by ideal-vs-smooth (valuation) points (Benard-Florens-Rodau) or by complex-length/cone-angle (Thurston Dehn-surgery space), while the project stratifies by meridian eigenvalue ORDER (infinite vs finite/root-of-unity). These coincide on the geometric component in many cases but are not definitionally the same; a specialist must confirm whether the project's order-stratification is genuinely distinct or a re-description of the valuation/ideal-point picture. COMPONENT INVERSION (R1/R2): in the n=3 Falbel data the tidy power relation L^3=M lives on the Dehn-filling components D2/D3, whereas the geometric component D1 carries a 141-polynomial relation — the project reads k on the geometric/loxodromic component, so whether [A,B]=s*mu^k with clean integer k actually holds on the geometric component for n>=3 (vs only on orbifold strata) needs explicit specialist check. RANK BARRIER: essentially all A-polynomial-slope machinery (CCGLS, Benard-Florens-Rodau, Baker-Petersen, Petersen, Hoste-Shanahan) is rank-2-only; the entire higher-n question lives outside the well-developed theory, so 'APPEARS-NOVEL' here partly reflects a genuine gap in the field rather than exhaustive coverage. SOURCE QUALITY: all load-bearing citations are primary peer-reviewed papers (arXiv + journal); the few semanticscholar-only claims were the ones REFUTED. TIME-SENSITIVITY: low — the n=3 results (2014-2016) and CCGLS framework (1994) are stable; the most recent relevant work (Daly 2024) is a preprint and could be superseded, but it does not assert the higher-n family either.
openQuestions: Does Zickert's SL(n,C) A-variety framework (arXiv:1405.0025, 'Ptolemy coordinates, Dehn invariant, and the A-polynomial', Math. Z. 2016) — which drops boundary-unipotency and is cited as independently producing the n=3 figure-eight A-polynomial — contain or readily yield the uniform L=(-1)^{n-1}M^n family for n>=4? This is the single most important unchecked prior-art source for R1's higher-n claim and was not directly examined.

Is the project's order(mu)-stratification (infinite-order/cusped vs finite-order/Dehn-filling-orbifold meridian) provably equivalent to, or genuinely finer/coarser than, the standard ideal-point-vs-smooth-point (valuation) stratification of Benard-Florens-Rodau and the Thurston complex-length/cone-angle stratification? Resolving this decides whether R2 is PARTIALLY-KNOWN (re-description) or APPEARS-NOVEL (distinct).

For n>=3 on the GEOMETRIC component specifically (not the Dehn-filling components D2/D3 where L^3=M lives), does an integer-exponent identity [A,B]=s*mu^k actually hold, given that Falbel reports the geometric component D1 carries a 141-polynomial non-tidy relation? This bears on whether the metallic exponent k is even well-defined as a single integer on the geometric stratum at higher n.

Does any higher-rank (SL(n), n>2) A-polynomial-slope or boundary-slope theory exist beyond the n=3 figure-eight computations — i.e., is there a Culler-Shalen / Newton-polygon analogue for SL(n,C) character varieties that could pre-empt R3's order-determined-slope claim? No such source surfaced, but the field gap means a representation-theory specialist should confirm before banking R3 as novel.

refuted.claim: The GTZ gluing-equations framework parametrizes BOUNDARY-UNIPOTENT (peripheral-unipotent / Borel) PGL(n,C) and SL(n,C) representations via shape/Ptolemy coordinates and Neumann-Zagier-type gluing+cusp equations. This is the unipotent-meridian regime, NOT the loxodromic-meridian geometric component on which the A-polynomial slope k and the identity [A,B]=s*mu^k live -- so the paper's machinery does not directly produce the metallic exponent.

The paper's only explicit figure-eight knot example is computed at n=3 (the gluing/cusp equations in Section 14 are written 'for n = 3'), yielding 4 zero-dimensional algebraic components over Q(sqrt(-7)) and Q(sqrt(-3)), with the geometric one arising from PSL(2,C)->SL(3,C). It does NOT present a higher-n (n>=4) figure-eight family, nor the A-polynomial relation L=(-1)^(n-1) M^n, nor punctured-torus-bundle / metallic (R^m L^m, m>1) generalizations.

The entire framework treats only BOUNDARY-UNIPOTENT representations (peripheral curves -> unipotent elements), so all peripheral/cusp eigenvalues are forced to 1. This structurally excludes the loxodromic-meridian (order(mu)=infinity, eigenvalue != root of unity) geometric stratum on which R2/R3 say the A-polynomial exponent k must be read - the paper cannot see eigenvalue-order strata at all.

General hyperbolic once-punctured torus bundles are parametrized by the Murasugi R/L word decomposition of the SL(2,Z) monodromy (phi = i^eps R^{n1} L^{n2} ... L^{n2k}), and the paper's worked example is phi = L^2 R^2 -- i.e. it explicitly handles a metallic-type R^m L^m bundle and treats the figure-eight (m=1) as the precursor case. This is direct prior art for the SETUP of the metallic once-punctured-torus family (R1), though it works in PGL(4,R) as the projective-deformation space of the PSL(2,C) holonomy, not as an SL(n) figure-eight A-polynomial tower.

The slopes of the sides of the Newton polygon of the A-polynomial are boundary slopes of incompressible surfaces in the knot complement. This is the central CCGLS [3] theorem and is exactly the 'A-polynomial slope = exponent k' linkage that R1/R3 invoke: the exponent k in [A,B]=s*mu^k is the A-polynomial slope, which CCGLS identify with a boundary slope.

The character/representation variety is stratified into honest representation points (the image πV) versus points at infinity (π̄V − πV), and the latter -- the ideal points reached by sequences of representations going to infinity -- are precisely what yield incompressible surfaces and hence the boundary slopes. This directly supports R2: the boundary-slope/exponent data is read off the going-to-infinity (ideal) stratum, distinguished from finite-character points.

For knot families obtained by Dehn filling a fixed parent manifold, the A-polynomial's defining (Ptolemy) equations split into a FIXED finite set of 'outside' equations plus a SEQUENCE of equations determined completely by the slope of the Dehn filling, yielding a recursive definition of the A-polynomial in terms of the filling slope. This is a slope/Dehn-filling stratification of the A-polynomial computation and is directly relevant to R2's claim about slope/Dehn-filling structure being standard.

The CCGLS/Culler-Long framework assigns to each ideal point of an SL(2,C) character-variety curve a root of unity lambda determined ORDER-theoretically (its order divides the number of boundary components of the associated incompressible surface), via tr_alpha(x) = lambda + lambda^{-1}; this is an arithmetic/order-based characterization of the boundary-trace at the degeneration, directly supporting R3's claim that the relevant exponent/spectral data is order-determined rather than rank-determined.

Ideal points are precisely the boundary (infinite-order / pole-of-peripheral-trace) points of the smooth projective model of the geometric component, and are operationally distinguished from finite-order representations: at an ideal point a peripheral trace function has a pole, forcing exactly one finite boundary slope. This is the standard CCGLS stratification of the character variety into ideal (cusped-degeneration) vs. interior (finite-order/Dehn-filling) points that R2 asks about.

There is a precise geometric stratification of points in Dehn surgery space by the complex length (boundary holonomy) of the peripheral curve: complex length 2*pi*i gives a smooth complete/filled structure (infinite-order meridian limit at the cusp), complex length alpha*i with alpha not 2*pi gives a hyperbolic CONE structure (orbifold / finite-order point) with cone angle alpha. This is exactly the cusped-vs-finite-order stratification by meridian holonomy that R2 concerns, expressed geometrically rather than via the A-polynomial.
